text: A sixty seven year old woman was referred to our department where we performed a colonoscopy to reveal an ulceral fungating mass at the cecum.
A gel point port is inserted through a 3.5 cm umbilical cutaneous incision, followed by the insertion of a 2 mm mini lap needle scopic grasper into the right lower quadrant area.
The surgeon performs primary vascular control with complete mesocolic excision and central vascular ligation along the right border of the superior mesenteric vein using the medial approach.
The surgeon exposes an avascular surgical plane along Toldt's line, revealing the underlying duodenum.
The intraoperative view shows several enlarged lymph nodes located in the right colic mesentery, adjacent to the central vascular trunk.
The surgeon performs lymph node dissection along the superior mesenteric artery and vein, carefully isolating and removing the enlarged lymph nodes from the surrounding vascular structures.
The surgeon ligates and transects the iliocolic vein at its root, where it drains into the superior mesenteric vein.
The surgeon transects the iliocolic artery at its root and dissects it upwards towards the middle colic vessels, following the established surgical plane.
The surgeon exposes an avascular surgical plane to reveal the head of the pancreas and the duodenum, highlighting the anatomical structures within the surgical field.
Following lymph node dissection around the middle colic vessels, the surgeon identifies the right branch of the middle colic artery, visible within the surgical field.
The surgeon ligates and transects the right branch of the middle colic artery at its root, completing the dissection around the middle colic vessels.
The surgeon incises the peritoneum in the retrocecal recess and mobilizes it laterally, revealing underlying structures.
The surgeon transects the greater omentum of the transverse colon and detaches its attachment at the hepatic flexure, revealing the underlying structures.

The final intraoperative view shows the surgical field after complete mesocolic excision and central vascular ligation for right-sided colon cancer, with clear exposure of the relevant anatomical structures.
A 2mm incision is made in the right lower quadrant to facilitate drainage.
The total procedure time was three hundred and ten minutes.
Pathologic examination of the specimen showed a four point five centimeter by four centimeter mass of moderately differentiated T four A N two B M zero adenocarcinomas.
The numbers of total and positive lymph
nodes harvested were thirty-three and eight, respectively.
Among the eight positive lymph nodes, there was one positive lymph node at the central area.
The proximal and distal reaction margins were thirty-four and twenty-two millimeters, respectively.
The patient was discharged on postoperative day nine.
Chial leakage occurred and was treated conservatively.
